mnemonics: ONION PEELING: Like peeling an onion layer by layer, a nested try statement handles exceptions from the inner layer (inner try) first, then the outer layers (outer try).
keyPoints: An outer try block can contain one or more inner try blocks, allowing for hierarchical exception handling.
If an inner try block does not have a catch handler for a particular exception, the exception propagates to the next outer try block.
Nested try blocks are useful for handling exceptions at different levels of code granularity, such as in a method call within another method.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly ordering catch blocks, placing more general exception handlers before more specific ones, which can lead to the wrong catch block executing.
Creating overly complex and deeply nested try structures that are difficult to read and maintain, instead of refactoring code into separate methods.
